item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to Baldi’s basics in education and learning! That’s me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I would assume the former one, since it's fabricating memories already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pardon if this's already been asked, but how does SCP 2979 affect those who hadn't had a physics teacher? Would a fabricated memory of one be made to supplement, or nothing would happen at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you everyone for +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Holloway: Yeah he did. He'd sing that one song, "Oh No There's Two of Them Now Please Make It Stop." Something like that. Really weird song, come to think of it.

This is where i upvoted. In spirit, since i was on my phone at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's great. You brought a gust of fresh air on infohazards. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got a +1 from me. I usually don't like overly confusing scips, but this rocks. I think what won me over was Mr. Redacted calling out for help via quotations in the interview logs. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
shit, I was really high when I read this and it fucked me up…

Erm, I mean, great article. I always like seeing infohazards, and what makes this one work is the twist on its nature. It's not AWCY piece that makes people a fish, it's scared and confused and hates what it does to people.

I would love to read a tale about this guy, and what he sees as reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. Disappointed I am. I thought it was the long-awaited apotheosis of a cruel vendetta! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really actually :p she was pretty cool. I'm not sure where I got the idea to have it be a physics teacher, just kinda pulled it out of nowhere.

Also glad to see the skip is fairing better now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, Eekium!

Yep. You've got yourself a pretty good skip now, my friend! Off to +100! And more!

Oh damn, I already upvoted…. ;-D

E.T.A.: okay, you can tell me now, we're all alone in here. Was your physics teacher so very awful? rrh rrh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "detached from reality" is actually a really good way to put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah HAH. Gooooood.

Correction to Interview 1: excellent
Correction to Interview 2: excellent

I still have one nitpick though. ;> A little one!!!!!!!

+ show block
– hide block

'Description' of the anomalous effect of any skip needs to be precise and accurate. You forgot to mention an important aspect of the anomaly, that also makes it a wee bit more dangerous than just change of personality:
1) the memories created by 2979 are nonsensical and contradictory
2) ergo, the long-term effect is … mmmmh… detachment from reality, or something.

So, I suggest:

Hosts will have memories fabricated around this teacher to support their belief.

could be followed by:
Hosts will have memories fabricated around this teacher to support their belief; those memories are nonsensical and often contradictory.

Consequence:

The way in which this happens is unpredictable, and varies from person to person, but most subjects exhibit difficulty focusing and will over time become delusional.

could become:
The way in which this happens is unpredictable, and varies from person to person, but most subjects exhibit difficulty focusing and will over time become delusional and noticeably detached from reality.
(or something! I'm not very happy with my 'detached from reality'…)

What say you?

Okay. Won't wait now: off to upvote!!!!! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(since I'm browsing recent comments)

I believe the author used a name generator linked on page 1 of the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No such place as Willowdale, MA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why I liked it better with the third interview log still in place. It did help to make this clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spelling and articulation, since I suppose you are going for something that normal humans cannot make up on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2979 is an infohazardous memetic infection which occurs upon perceiving (directly or indirectly) the name "Mr. [REDACTED]"1.

Footnotes
1. This name is composed of symbolic characters not found in any known system of graphemes and when spoken, pronounced with a combination of phonemes not found in any known language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think really, when it comes right down to it, the spelling is part of the anomalous properties (probably should just find a way to overtly state that in the article).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that the implication is that he therefore doesn't have any family. Or at least no family with the same surname. You can't logically share a name with anybody else and also have a name written in totally unique characters, after all. He might never have physically existed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I find this explanation hard to swallow, unless "no one will ever come up with this accidentally" is part of the effects. Meaning the Foundation has killed this guy's family or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Why didn't I think of that. Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: then [REDACT] the name instead of blackboxing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, again, the implication I'm trying (and failing, unfortunately) to make is that this name is one that- whatever it is- is impossible to accidentally come up with on your own. It may be made of sounds and letters completely foreign to human experience, something we literally are incapable of imagining because they are outside of our experience of what sound and language is.

Nobody can share the name,or accidentally come up with it, in the same way that no one can accidentally create a totally new color. Things like X-Rays can be seen by some animals, but not us, and we cannot even guess as to how it looks because it's so far outside of our experience. This name functions in a similar manner, in that it's so alien to us that we don't even have the capacity to accidentally come up with it by chance, no matter how many words or sounds we mix together.

In other words, it's not like Mr. Smith. It'd be closer to Mr. \°§!», only written with completely new symbols not present in any language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem also surfaces with people sharing that name. For your statement that there's only one vector left at this time to make sense, you'd have to have a name that is sufficiently unique to never have been chosen before. Since you've chosen to make it just the last name, it's going to be really hard convincing your reader of the plausibility of that claim (that there's only one vector left).

but the name is described as being "one of those names you have to hear or see in order to say in the first place, you know the ones. The kind of name nobody can just come up with on their own."

You only need to perceive it though, it's just a certain order of characters, whether or not you can parse them is completely irrelevant according to your article. Now, if hearing the name is also enough to spread the memetic infection, that really makes your statement about the last remaining vector implausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the teacher exists, but only within those memories

It took me a while to grok this. Perhaps make it a tiny weeny bit clearer? (it can also be I'm rather dense!!!)

It's as if he's trapped in all these memories against his will, and is simply shouting outside for help to get it to stop.

You should use this, IMO. Not only would it make the whole thing clearer, but it's also a good image!

I really don't think you need this Foundation reference - our article is 'rich' enough without it.

I'm gonna try to do some revisions and hopefully that'll sort it out

<Eta grabs a book and plunks down into a comfortable armchair> I'll be waiting! ;-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea I had (and I tried to get across, but possibly I did it poorly) is that the teacher exists, but only within those memories. He may or may not have been an actual teacher originally, but it's impossible to tell because any records of him are unreliable, as they'd be written by infected people. As for the teacher's reference to The Foundation, it's because he has some agency within the outside world. It's as if he's trapped in all these memories against his will, and is simply shouting outside for help to get it to stop.

Also, The Foundation ended up containing the initial outbreak rather quickly, before it could escalate. If they hadn't, it could have been potentially disastrous.

(Although now that you mention it, that doesn't explain why he knows The Foundation's name)

I may have done a not so good job of explaining this in the skip, but I'm gonna try to do some revisions and hopefully that'll sort it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused as to how you can contain an outbreak of a name? Even if it has to be used to refer to this specific person (which isn't specified), he would be leaving a trail of hazardous documents from the day he was born. So it should really be Euclid at least.

All the same, the story is interesting enough for a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eekium: I corrected 'instances has been' into 'have been' (3rd paragraph of description). It was bothering me no end, and I don't think there's any other mistake, so - problem solved! ;>

Okay. I'm torn on this one.
On one hand, it's a good 'WTF???' SCP article, the kind I like. The absurd memories of infected people are both funny and… um… sad? disturbing? You know!
I suspect someone around here did not like his/her physics teacher…. rrh rrh

On the other hand.
<thinks hard and rereads the skip>
Yeah: I have a problem with chronology. In fact, I have a few problems:

+ show block
– hide block

How long has the teacher's name had this anomalous effect? How many people were infected before the Foundation stepped in and contained it? DID they manage to step in before the one-month deadline was over for the first infected?
A teacher's name in a high school is used A LOT, orally and in writing. And for each infected person within the high school, there can be several infected out of it (friends, parents, family, authorities, local neswpaper….). So, how come the whole area/State/country/world is not infected by now?
OR IS IT???? ;>

I also don't much like the (teacher's?) reference to the Foundation: it doesn't make much sense, even within a WTF article!

Is the teacher still teaching? If he is, shouldn't he be retired and contained? If he isn't, why no word about him in the containment procedures? Why didn't / doesn't he simply change his name? Did the anomaly of his name only activate when he became a teacher? Oh no, wait, he's not real, right? Now I'm confused. Or infected!!!!

I'm right here on the verge of upvoting… but not quite doing it yet. Can you do something about this uncomfortable position, Eekium? ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember Mr. ████. There was this one time he took our class scuba diving and told us: "You should upvote SCP-2979 because it was quite cool to read."

What a cool guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of weird that makes me keep coming back to this site. GG

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'll see if I can coax that greatness out with some editing (it's worth a shot!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might not have explaine this too well, and I'll see if I can make it more obvious when I get a chance to do more revisions tomorrow, but the name is described as being "one of those names you have to hear or see in order to say in the first place, you know the ones. The kind of name nobody can just come up with on their own."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the name is either esoteric or uncommon enough for it not to be chosen. It's possible, but I'm guessing not anytime in the near future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr. δҖQ4?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviews are quite great but the bits about personality changes seem unnecessary and don't really pay off. Novote for now, but it'll be easily swayed by some edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that really the case, though? If, say, someone writing a book was to randomly choose Mr. ████'s name for a character (the name is only 4 letters long, after all, it's not that unlikely), wouldn't that cause a new vector to spontaneously be created?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's only one vector of infection, and if you leave it alone in a locker, no one will be affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I just read two articles with this exact same effect, and I don't need a third. Also, how is this safe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've done what I can to fix those issues. Any suggestions on how to follow expand on the idea? I've been thinking on how I could make the article have a good payoff or satisfying ending but I can't think of any good ways to improve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to prove you can't please everyone, I thought it was better WITH the third log. For me, the logs are really what make this skip. Prior to that, it's just another info-hazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Better article overall, so I no longer want to downvote, though I'm not fully convinced of it. I see what you're going for and think the idea is sound, but some parts feel weird and awkward:

This fact remains unchanged even if an individual comes from a region where the term "12th grade" is not applicable.

This feels like an attempt to shoehorn American school systems in the anomalous effects it has on people, regardless of where they come from. Can't they just assume that Mr. ████ is their physics teacher for whatever their equivalent of 12th Grade is?

This consequently leads to a personality change within the host.

I find this whole bit weird, and it's unclear if its due to the memories being altered or Mr. ████ trying to have some effect on the individual to not be tormented by this. If it's the former; do personality changes really need to be the end result? If the latter, why would he do something to that extent?

And my issue with skips that talk about an individual's trauma by focusing on the byproduct of that (like SCP-1467) is that we don't know much about the individual, even though the article is really about them. However, unlike that one (which I really like), this doesn't explore his character much, and highlights the effects it has on those infected. It leaves me very wanting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting. The description is at first a bit boring, until the end where it's revealed he's aware of his infohazardous status. I was hoping for some kind of Dr. Gideon payoff but it never came. Downvoted, but there's a lot of potential here for something great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done and done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Both issues fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last interview could be cut, honestly.

Also, can you add spaces in-between each line in the interview, so it's not one big wall of text? Like this:

Dr. Holloway: Do you remember what he looked like?

D-10380: I don't remember, but Mr. ████ wasn't round.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Watched until 2017, eh? Waiting for 2016 to end? Was Mr. ████ teaching 12th grade physics forty years prior to the latter date, perchance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost feel bad saying it, but I kind of wish the entry ended with the first interview. While I enjoyed reading the next two (especially seeing how quickly the memetic infection kicks in), I feel like most of that information was already kind of established, and Interview 1 just ends with a heck of kick, and to me, is a much more solid and punchy ending. Still upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this skip is a good idea, there are a number of problems with the execution. If you fix the issues I would probably upvote, but presently this skip earns a downvote.

+ show issues
– hide block

The high school SCP-2979 was first identified in is to be watched for further anomalous activity until 01/01/17

This is a very awkward sentence. Later you state but then redact the name of the school:

… was recovered from ██████ ███ High School.

I realize that you are usually not supposed to redact sections of the Containment Procedures in general, however, this skip would be better off having the name redacted in the Procedures than keeping the awkward wording you currently have.
However, better than either of those is to just leave the name in the clear. And if you aren't comfortable using the name of a real school, just make one up or pick it at random:

Grand Ridge High School in Aethelney, MI is to be watched for further anomalous activity until 01/01/17.

See, mutch better. And this is just something I pulled at random from http://fantasynamegenerators.com/school-names.php and http://fantasynamegenerators.com/town_names.php.

This unanimously leads to a personality change within the host.

That is not what unanimously means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. While I think Infohazards (in general) are overdone, Mr. ████'s obvious frustration and the way he manages to communicate via the false memories is very original. Take your +1. :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed! (I think)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's pretty irrelevant. Changed it to something that hopefully provides a contradiction and also is far more believable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-10380 was interviewed one day prior to this, in which he explained his science class was always too noisy (and evidently full of fish) for Mr. ████ to ever speak.

While I do get that you're saying "the D-class' recounting of the SCP" is different everyday, I thought you could have changed it to show two interviews where it expresses contradictory accounts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, except for the "fish" comment which seems to come out of nowhere and not be relevant to anything?
